A full brand identity for a cycling café with ambitions beyond the café. Built to grow from Johannesburg to the World Tour.

01 Manifesto
Souplesse Brand Identity 2026 · Designsbydos
Souplesse Brand Identity
Section 01

Manifesto

The Rider · The Café · The City

This is not a brand statement. It is a point of view. Why Souplesse exists, who it exists for, and what it believes about cycling, coffee, and what a city like Johannesburg deserves.

  • 7.1 The Ride
  • 7.2 The Café
  • 7.3 The City
The road asks
nothing of you
except that you show up.
7.1 — The Ride
Nobody
rides for
easy.

You clip in before the sun comes up. You do the intervals when your legs say no. You ride in the rain because the alternative is not riding at all — and that is worse. Cycling is not a hobby. It is a practice. A daily negotiation between what you think you can do and what your body discovers it already knows.

Souplesse is a French word for suppleness — the kind of smooth, effortless power that takes years to build and never feels like effort when you finally find it. That is what every rider is working towards. That is what we named ourselves after.

7.2 — The Café
Before
and
after.

Every ride has two moments that matter most. The one before — when you are nervous, cold, not quite awake, and the coffee is the thing that makes it possible. And the one after — when the legs are spent, the conversation starts, and you remember why you do this with other people.

Souplesse was built for both. Not as a pit stop. Not as an afterthought. As the place the ride begins and returns to. We take the coffee as seriously as you take the kilometres. We take the space as seriously as the sport.

7.3 — The City
Johan
nesburg.

Johannesburg does not do things quietly. It does not wait for permission. It builds what it needs, finds the gaps, and fills them with something better than what was there before. The cycling community here runs on exactly that energy — early starts, long roads, and a city that rewards the people willing to get up and take it.

This is not a café that happened to end up in Johannesburg. This is a café that could only exist here. The road is the same for everyone. Show up.

Ride.
Return.
Repeat.

We are not the destination. We are the beginning and the end of every ride. The coffee that gets you out the door and the table that waits when you come back. The door is open at 05:30 because that is when it needs to be open. Not because it is convenient. Because this is what the riders here asked for — and Souplesse exists to answer.

Souplesse Cycling Café — Johannesburg, South Africa — Est. 2026
02 Brand Strategy + Voice
Souplesse Brand Identity 2026 · Designsbydos
Souplesse Brand Identity
Section 02

BrandStrategy

Before a single mark is drawn, a brand must know exactly what problem it exists to solve, who it serves, and what it stands for. Every decision that follows traces back to this page.

  • 1.1 The Problem
  • 1.2 The Audience
  • 1.3 Positioning
  • 1.4 Brand Values
  • 1.5 The Single Line
  • 1.6 Mission Roadmap
1.1
The
Problem.
Problem Statement

Road cycling in South Africa is home to extraordinary talent and extraordinary terrain. What it lacks is a culture with an open door. A space where the full tradition of the sport lives, but entry feels like an invitation rather than an audition. Right now, an entire generation of potential riders never finds out what they are missing.

Root 01

A Belonging Problem

The culture signals insider status at every touchpoint. The kit, the language, the group dynamics. New riders feel like they are being tested before they have had a chance to begin.

Root 02

An Identity Problem

South African cycling has no unifying brand that reflects its own character. Modern, energetic, open. It borrows its identity from European tradition without adding anything of its own to the conversation.

Root 03

A Pipeline Problem

Without a place to gather, to start, and to develop, talent stays isolated rather than networked. Individual riders never compound into a culture. The sport stays small when it doesn't have to be.

1.2

The Audience

Primary

The Curious Non-Rider

Ages 18 to 34

Has seen cycling culture from the outside and felt something pull at them. The early mornings, the community, the physical ambition. Never found a way in that didn't feel intimidating. Coffee is the entry point. The riding comes later, on its own terms.

Urban, active, culturally engaged Already takes coffee seriously Exercises but hasn't found their sport yet Responds to good design and real authenticity
Secondary

The Developing Rider

Ages 16 to 28

Already riding. Strava, weekend group rides, maybe a local race or two. Passionate but not yet embedded in a real community. Looking for the place where the culture lives, not just the sport. Needs mentorship more than they would ever admit to anyone.

Owns a decent bike and rides regularly Socially motivated, rides for the people Aspirational about the sport's traditions The future of the racing team
Tertiary

The Committed Rider

Ages 28 to 50

Serious cyclist. Training plans, multiple bikes, race entries. Already has souplesse and knows it. Comes for the coffee and stays for the community. The anchor of the culture and the person the developing rider is quietly trying to become one day.

Races or has raced. Knows the tradition. High value customer and genuine brand advocate The mentor the community actually needs Brings credibility to the space
The Design Principle

All three audiences must sit at the same table without hierarchy. The design works for each of them at once: deep enough for the committed rider, open enough for the curious non-rider, energetic enough for the developing rider. Nobody is more welcome than anyone else.

1.3

Positioning

For anyone who has felt something watching a cyclist disappear over a hill at dawn, Souplesse is the place that turns that feeling into a life. We carry the full tradition of road cycling and extend it, without condition, to everyone.
Heritage
Modern
Exclusive
Inclusive
Café
Club
Casual
Performance
0

The Landscape

Where Souplesse sits

Rapha Premium heritage. Deep culture. High barrier to entry. Aspirational but cold. Exclusive
The Clubhouse Modern minimal design. Community focus. Still reads elite in practice. Insider
Generic Cycle Café Approachable but no soul. Coffee as fuel. No cultural ambition. Generic
SOUPLESSE Full cultural depth. Unconditional welcome. Modern energy. South African. Ours
1.4

Brand Values

Value 01

Open Door

The depth of cycling culture belongs to anyone willing to show up. No prerequisites. No knowledge test at the door. You earn your understanding through experience, not before it. The invitation is unconditional and it stays that way.

In practice: every ride has a sweep rider. every question gets a real answer. every level is visible in the group.
Value 02

Full Depth

We don't simplify the sport to make it accessible. The full tradition stays intact. The European soul, the obsessive coffee culture, the suffering and the joy. Openness does not mean shallowness. We make room for everyone to grow into it.

In practice: the coffee is seriously sourced. the routes are real. the kit is proper. the culture is genuine.
Value 03

Flow Forward

Souplesse is the pursuit of flow state on the bike. Everything points in one direction: toward the moment when effort disappears and movement becomes pure. That applies to the coffee, the rides, the community, and the brand itself.

In practice: we measure success in new riders who stay, not in customers who pass through.
1.5 — The Single Line

Cycling
is for
everyone.

Not a slogan. The entire brand strategy in four words. Every design decision, every product choice, every ride invitation gets held against this line and either serves it or it doesn't.

SOUPLESSE — Cycling Café — South Africa / Brand Foundation Statement / Section 02
1.6

Mission Roadmap

Phase One — Now

The Café

Year 01 to 02
Coffee programme, sourced and roasted Weekly community rides, all levels First kit drop — jersey, bib, bidon Social presence, ride recaps, culture content
Phase Two

The Community

Year 02 to 03
Youth development programme Brand partnerships in gear and nutrition Structured training groups by level Regional ride events and sportives
Phase Three

The Team

Year 03 to 05
Souplesse Racing — continental entry Sponsored rider development National race programme SA cycling federation partnerships
Phase Four

The Legacy

Year 05 and beyond
International race presence Second location and franchise model SA talent on global stages The sport changed. The door stayed open.

Brand Essence

Brand Name SOUPLESSE French cycling term for smooth, effortless power. Cycling's word for flow state.
Category Cycling Café and Community The first touchpoint in a larger mission to grow cycling culture in South Africa.
Home Cape Town, South Africa Headquartered here. Built for everywhere.
The Single Line Cycling is for everyone.

Brand Character

Personality Serious. Warm. Specific. Like a rider who has done the hard kilometres and has nothing left to prove to anyone.
Tone Direct, Dry, Inviting Never loud. Never ironic. Always means exactly what it says.
Design Language Swiss Precision and Electric Energy The discipline of a Swiss grid. The energy of a hi-viz vest at 05:30 in the morning.
The Problem Solved Belonging Not aesthetics. Not visibility. The feeling that the door is genuinely open to you.
2.6

Voice Principles

The brand voice is not a tone of voice guide. It is a set of convictions. Every piece of writing — from a menu description to a race day post — should be able to trace itself back to one of these four principles. If it can't, rewrite it.

01
Earned
Confidence

We do not shout. We do not perform enthusiasm. Souplesse speaks like someone who has put in the kilometres — quietly certain, never needing to prove it. Confidence comes from the work, not from the volume.

02
Open
Door

The café is for everyone who is curious about cycling, not just those already deep in it. We never use language that creates an in-group and an out-group. Technical terms are welcomed — but never wielded as gatekeeping.

03
Direct
Not Blunt

We say what we mean in as few words as possible. Short sentences. Active verbs. No filler. But directness is never coldness — there is always a human being on the other end of every word we write.

04
Local
Soul

Souplesse is South African. The cycling culture we are building is rooted here — in our roads, our climbs, our community. The voice reflects that. It is not trying to sound European or American. It knows where it is from.

2.7

Tone by Context

The voice does not change. The register does. Think of it like a person — the same individual speaks differently in a race briefing than at a coffee counter than in a condolence card. Same character, different mode.

Context 01
Everyday Café & Social
Register — Warm · Informal · Present

"The legs were heavy this morning. The espresso fixed that. Come in."

Short, human, present tense. Written like a person, not a brand. Acknowledges the shared reality of riding and recovering. Invites without overselling.

Context 02
Race Day & Events
Register — Sharp · Charged · Decisive

"No excuses today. The road is the same for everyone. We'll be here when you get back."

Energy goes up. Sentences get shorter. The café positions itself as part of the ritual — the send-off and the return. Still warm, but the warmth has edge on race day.

Context 03
New Riders & Community
Register — Patient · Generous · Honest

"You don't need to know everything about cycling to belong here. You just need to be curious about it."

This is where the open door principle matters most. No jargon. No assumptions. No condescension. Just an honest invitation into something worth caring about.

Context 04
Brand & Editorial
Register — Considered · Precise · Principled

"Souplesse is a French cycling term. It means efficiency of movement — the ability to ride hard without looking like you are. That is the standard we hold ourselves to."

Longer, more deliberate. This is the voice of the brand explaining itself — in the manifesto, in interviews, in the brand guidelines themselves. Careful with every word.

Context 05
Signage & Environmental
Register — Minimal · Declarative · Still

"Ride. Return. Repeat."

Physical space demands extreme brevity. Three words or fewer. Declarative. These are not instructions — they are invitations compressed to their essential form.

Context 06
Menu & Product
Register — Sensory · Unhurried · Honest

"Double shot. Served at 65°C. No foam. No performance."

The menu voice is stripped back and specific. We describe what something actually is, not how it makes you feel. Trust the product. Let it speak.

2.8

Writing in Practice

The best way to understand the voice is to see it beside what it is not. Each row shows the same piece of content — once written in the Souplesse voice, once written the way most brands would write it.

Souplesse Voice
Generic Brand Voice
Social caption — morning ride

"Some rides are about the data. Some rides are just about getting out. This morning was the second kind."

Social caption — morning ride

"Rise and grind! Nothing beats an early morning ride to get those endorphins flowing! Who else crushed it this morning? Drop a comment below!"

Tagline

"Where cyclists come before the ride and after."

Tagline

"Fuelling Your Passion for Cycling — Coffee, Community & More!"

Menu — espresso description

"Single origin. Pulled long. No milk unless you ask."

Menu — espresso description

"A beautifully crafted espresso made from the finest ethically sourced single-origin beans, roasted to perfection for a rich, complex flavour journey."

New rider — welcome message

"You don't need a race number to belong here. Just curiosity and a willingness to show up."

New rider — welcome message

"Welcome to our amazing cycling community! Whether you're a seasoned pro or just starting your cycling journey, there's a place for YOU at Souplesse!"

2.9

Words We Own

These are the words and phrases that belong to the Souplesse vocabulary. They carry the brand's convictions. Use them often, use them correctly, and resist diluting them with synonyms that carry less weight.

Words We Own
Souplesse

Efficiency of movement. Effort that does not show. The ideal state of every ride — and every interaction with the brand.

Flow

The state where effort and ease become the same thing. Not a marketing word — a real experience that every cyclist and every good café knows.

The Ride

Specific, with a capital R when written formally. Not "a ride" or "your ride." The ride is the shared thing that connects everyone who walks through the door.

Return

Coming back — to the café, to the road, to the group. The word carries the promise that Souplesse is a constant. You will always have somewhere to return to.

Show Up

The only requirement. Not fast, not experienced, not equipped with the right gear. Just present. Showing up is the beginning of everything.

Words We Avoid
Passion

Overused to the point of meaning nothing. Every brand claims passion. We demonstrate ours through specificity, not declaration.

Amazing

Vague positive filler. If something is worth describing, describe it specifically. "Amazing espresso" tells you nothing. "Pulled long, served at 65°C" tells you everything.

Community

Not because it is wrong — but because it is overused by brands who do not actually have one. We build community through action. We do not announce it.

Journey

The most overused word in brand writing. Avoided entirely. We talk about rides, roads, climbs, efforts, returns. Not journeys.

Lifestyle

Cycling is not a lifestyle product. It is a discipline, a sport, a culture, a community. "Lifestyle" flattens it into something you buy. We reject that framing entirely.

2.10

What We Never Sound Like

These are not just bad writing habits. They are voice violations — each one contradicts a core principle of what Souplesse stands for. If a piece of copy sounds like any of these, it is not Souplesse copy.

Violation 01
The Hype Machine
"The MOST EPIC cycling café in South Africa is HERE and we are SO EXCITED to share this INCREDIBLE journey with our AMAZING community!!!"

Exclamation marks, superlatives, and capitalised enthusiasm are the sound of a brand that does not trust its product. Souplesse trusts its product. The writing reflects that.

Violation 02
The Gatekeeper
"Real cyclists know that a proper recovery starts with the right macros. If you're still counting calories you're probably not training hard enough."

We never use knowledge or effort as a measure of belonging. Cycling culture has an exclusion problem. Souplesse exists specifically to solve it — not to replicate it.

Violation 03
The Corporate
"At Souplesse, our mission is to leverage the cycling ecosystem to deliver best-in-class experiences that align with our core values of community, excellence, and passion."

Mission statements and corporate language belong in boardrooms, not brand communications. We write like a person, not a company. Always.

Violation 04
The Apologist
"We hope you don't mind but we just wanted to share a small update! Sorry for any inconvenience and please do let us know if this is okay!"

Earned confidence means never apologising for existing or communicating. We are not an inconvenience. We say what we have to say and we mean it.

Violation 05
The Poser
"We are stoked to be dropping some next-level content for all you rad cycling fam out there. Stay woke and keep shredding those KOMs, legends."

Borrowed slang from other subcultures signals that the brand does not have its own. Souplesse has its own voice. It does not need to borrow anyone else's.

Violation 06
The Vague Inspirer
"Chase your dreams. Live your best life. Because every ride is a new beginning and you are capable of more than you know."

Motivational poster copy replaces specific meaning with generic positivity. We inspire through precision — a well-described climb, a specific truth about the road. Not through platitudes.

03 Logo System
Souplesse Brand Identity 2026 · Designsbydos
Souplesse Brand Identity
Section 03

LogoSystem

The mark is the word itself. No illustration. No icon. No wheel or coffee cup. The name earns its own visual identity through weight, proportion, and one precise decision about where colour lives in the letterform.

  • 3.1 Concept and Rationale
  • 3.2 Primary Mark
  • 3.3 Variations and Lockup System
  • 3.4 Colour Applications
  • 3.5 Clear Space and Minimum Size
  • 3.6 What Never To Do
2.1 — Concept and Rationale

The logo is a typographic mark. No pictogram, no wheel, no coffee cup. Cycling's visual heritage is built on powerful condensed typography: race number bibs, stage winner boards, team car lettering. We inherit that tradition without quoting it directly.

The strongest brands trust their name to carry the meaning. Rapha doesn't show a bike. Nike doesn't show a shoe. Souplesse doesn't show a cyclist. The mark is the word. The word is the brand. Everything else is noise.

Three decisions that define the mark
01
Barlow Condensed Black

Maximum weight, maximum compression. The same typographic logic as a race number biб or a velodrome scoreboard. It commands space without needing size.

02
The Acid Terminal

The final E carries the accent in Acid Green. One character, one colour change. It marks the finish line of the word, the last beat of the pedal stroke. Remove it and the mark loses its pulse.

03
No Illustration Required

The temptation is always to show what the brand does. We resist it completely. You do not explain souplesse. You experience it. The mark works the same way.

3.2

Primary Mark

Mark anatomy
SOUPLESSE
A
The Typeface

Barlow Condensed Black, 900 weight. Set in all capitals, tracked at default. The letterforms are never stretched, compressed, or manually kerned beyond the typeface's native spacing. The proportions are already correct.

B
The Body

Eight letters set in the primary colour of the current background. On blue: white. On acid: carbon. On white: carbon. On carbon: white. The body never appears in a mid-tone colour. It always sits in maximum contrast to the ground.

E
The Terminal Accent

The final E is always set in Acid Green (#CCFF00) regardless of the background, with one exception: on the acid background itself, the terminal E shifts to Signal Blue. The accent is the only variable element in the entire mark. Nothing else changes.

Primary Lockup in use
Cycling Café
SOUPLESSE

"Cycling Café" in Barlow Condensed Bold, left edge aligned with the S of the wordmark. Used when context is needed — signage, packaging, the first impression of a new audience. When the brand is established, the wordmark stands alone.

3.3

Lockup System

Primary Lockup — Horizontal
Cycling Café
SOUPLESSE

The primary lockup. "Cycling Café" in Barlow Condensed Bold, left edge aligned with the S. Used on packaging, kit, signage, menus, and social. Drop the descriptor below 32px wordmark size and use the wordmark alone.

Wordmark Only
SOUPLESSE

The wordmark without descriptor. Used once the brand is established in context — on kit, cups, packaging, social. The name carries itself.

Stacked — Primary
Cycling Café
SOUPLESSE

Centred axis with flanking dashes. Left dash aligns to centre of O, right to centre of last S — a fixed proportional rule. Used on bidons, large signage, and display applications.

Badge — Medium and Small
Cycling Café
SOUPLESSE
Medium
Cycling Café
SOUPLESSE
Small

Full-width top rule creates a closed rectangular badge shape. One visual object — scales down to stickers, helmet decals, stamps, and embroidery patches without losing coherence.

Lettermark
S.
On Blue
S.
On Acid
S.
On Carbon

Used only at very small sizes — espresso cups, embroidery, favicon. The period is always a contrasting colour: Signal Blue on acid and carbon, Acid Green on blue and white. Never replaces the full wordmark at legible sizes.

3.4

Colour Applications

On Signal Blue
SOUPLESSE
White body / Acid terminal
#FFFFFF on #0052FF

Primary brand application. Used on packaging, social headers, and the dominant face of all printed materials.

On Acid Green
SOUPLESSE
Carbon body / Blue terminal
#0A0A0A on #CCFF00

High energy application. Used on kit, event materials, and anywhere maximum visibility is needed.

On White
SOUPLESSE
Carbon body / Blue terminal
#0A0A0A on #F8F8F4

Clean application for print documents, menus, and editorial contexts. The terminal shifts to Blue to maintain the colour logic.

On Carbon
SOUPLESSE
White body / Acid terminal
#F8F8F4 on #0A0A0A

Night mode and merchandise. Used on dark jerseys, dark packaging, and screen applications in dark mode.

The Colour Rule

The terminal E is always a colour accent. It is never the same colour as the body of the wordmark. On blue and carbon, the terminal is Acid Green. On acid and white, the terminal is Signal Blue. This rule has no exceptions.

2.5 — Clear Space

Breathing Room

The minimum clear space around the wordmark on all sides equals the cap height of the E — referred to as the x-unit. No other graphic elements, text, or edges may enter this zone at any size or orientation.

x
SOUPLESSE
Key
E
x = clear space unit Cap height of the E
in the wordmark

The minimum clear space on all sides equals the cap height of the E. No other graphic elements, text, or edges may enter this zone at any size.

Minimum Sizes

Size Scale

The wordmark must remain fully legible at all times. Below the minimum digital size of 120px wide, switch to the initial mark S. Below 40px, the mark is not used at all.

240px+
SOUPLESSE
Preferred
160px
SOUPLESSE
Good
120px
SOUPLESSE
Minimum
80px
SOUPLESSE
Too small
3.6

What Never To Do

Souplesse
Rule 01
Never Set in Mixed Case

The wordmark is always all capitals. Mixed case or title case removes the architectural quality of the letterforms and makes the mark look like a casual typesetting choice rather than a brand decision.

SOUPLESSE
Rule 02
Never Use Gradients or Effects

No gradients, no drop shadows, no glows, no outlines, no embossing. The mark is a flat typographic object. Any visual treatment applied to it competes with the letterforms and weakens the mark immediately.

SOUPLESSE
Rule 03
Never Add Illustration

No wheels, no cyclists, no coffee cups, no mountains, no cobblestones alongside the mark. The wordmark is complete on its own. Adding illustration dilutes the brand and contradicts the entire design philosophy.

SOUPLESSE
Rule 04
Never Lose the Accent

The terminal E is always a contrasting colour. A single-colour version of the wordmark only appears in situations of extreme constraint, such as single-colour printing. In all other contexts, the accent is non-negotiable.

S O U P L E S S E
Rule 05
Never Over-Track

The wordmark is set at its native tracking. Increasing the letter-spacing beyond the default destroys the compressed quality that gives the mark its authority. The density is the point.

SOUPLESSE
Rule 06
Never Stretch or Distort

The wordmark is never scaled non-uniformly. Horizontal stretching, vertical compression, or skewing the letterforms for any reason is not permitted. Scale uniformly or not at all.

2 — Logo System / Complete

The mark
is the word.
Trust it.

The strongest thing about this logo is what it doesn't do. It doesn't explain the brand, illustrate the sport, or decorate itself into memorability. It states the name with total conviction and gets out of the way. That restraint is the design.

04 Visual Language
Souplesse Brand Identity 2026 · Designsbydos
Souplesse Brand Identity
Section 04

VisualLanguage

Two chromatic brand colours. That is the entire palette. Signal Blue and Acid Green — one demands authority, one demands attention. White, Carbon, and the neutral scale are the grounds on which these two perform. Remove the grounds and the colours still exist. Remove the colours and the brand disappears.

  • 4.1Primary Colours
  • 4.2Neutral Scale
  • 4.3Colour Relationships
  • 4.4Typeface Rationale
  • 4.5Type Hierarchy
  • 4.6Typesetting Rules
3.1

Primary Colours

The palette was chosen for its refusal to be subtle. Signal Blue and Acid Green are the two chromatic brand colours — the colours that make Souplesse identifiable. White, Carbon, and the neutral scale are grounds: they exist to make the two brand colours perform. You could change the ground and the brand survives. Change the chromatic pair and it doesn't.

Signal
Blue
HEX   #0052FF
RGB   0 · 82 · 255
CMYK  100 · 68 · 0 · 0
PANTONE  285 C

The dominant brand colour. It carries authority without aggression. Strong enough to own a jersey, refined enough to own a menu. Signal Blue is always the foundation — never a background colour in the passive sense. It is a statement.

Acid
Green
HEX   #CCFF00
RGB   204 · 255 · 0
CMYK  20 · 0 · 100 · 0
PANTONE  381 C

The accent and the provocation. It is the terminal E. It is the descriptor text. It is the first thing the eye lands on in any composition. Acid Green is never used as a background for large type — it exists to cut through, not to fill space.

3.2

Neutral Scale

The neutrals are not a compromise. Carbon, Ink, and Stone are the grounds on which the primary colours perform. Off-White is the only alternative to black for large areas of light content. These five tones are the full supporting cast — nothing else is permitted.

Off-White
#F8F8F4
Light grounds
Print body text
Menu backgrounds
Stone
#1C1C1C
Section backgrounds
Card grounds
Subtle depth
Ink
#111111
Primary dark ground
Screen interfaces
Dark packaging
Carbon
#0A0A0A
Deepest background
Document ground
Night contexts
Mist
#888888
Supporting text
Captions
Metadata only
Print Note

Carbon, Ink, and Stone are screen-first surfaces. For standard print applications — menus, stationery, packaging inserts — use Off-White as the primary ground with Carbon body text and Signal Blue as accent. Full-bleed dark and Signal Blue backgrounds are suited to high-end offset print on quality stock only. When in doubt, print light.

3.3

Colour Relationships

Every combination in the system has a defined hierarchy. The primary colour always carries the wordmark body. The accent colour always carries the terminal E. These relationships do not change — only the backgrounds change.

Blue ground — Primary
SOUPLESSE

White wordmark body, Acid Green terminal. Maximum contrast on blue. The most used combination across all touchpoints — packaging, kit, signage, social.

Acid ground — High Energy
SOUPLESSE

Carbon wordmark body, Signal Blue terminal. The terminal shifts to blue because the E cannot appear on its own background colour. Used on race day, events, high-visibility kit.

White ground — Editorial
SOUPLESSE

Carbon wordmark body, Signal Blue terminal. Used on print documents, menus, white packaging. The terminal is always blue on light grounds — never acid on white, which loses contrast.

Carbon ground — Night
SOUPLESSE

White wordmark body, Acid Green terminal. Used on dark merchandise, dark packaging, and screen interfaces in dark mode. Mirrors the blue ground logic.

The Colour Rule

The terminal E is always the contrasting brand colour to the wordmark body. On blue and carbon grounds, the body is white and the terminal is Acid Green. On acid and white grounds, the body is carbon and the terminal is Signal Blue. This rule has no exceptions.

3.4

What Never To Do

The palette is tight by design. Every violation weakens the system. These are not suggestions.

SOUPLESSE
No Gradients

The palette is always flat and solid. Gradients between brand colours create a hybrid that belongs to neither. The colours must remain distinct — not blended.

SOUPLESSE
No Off-Palette Colours

No purple. No teal. No warm neutrals. No additional brand colours introduced for campaigns or seasons. The palette does not expand — it only deepens.

SOUPLESSE
No Acid on Acid

The terminal E is never set in Acid Green on an Acid Green ground. The contrast collapses. On any acid background, the terminal always shifts to Signal Blue without exception.

SOUPLESSE
No Tints or Opacity

Signal Blue and Acid Green are never used at reduced opacity or as tints. The palette values are absolute. A 60% blue is not a brand colour — it is a mistake.

SOUPLESSE
No Outlined Type

The wordmark is never rendered as an outline or stroke. Acid Green is not used as a stroke colour on the wordmark body — only as a solid fill on the terminal E.

SOUPLESSE
No Colour Blocks Within Type

Highlight effects, colour blocking behind individual letters, or split colour fills through the wordmark body are not permitted. The terminal E is the only colour variation within the mark.

4.4

Typography

Three typefaces, each with a defined role. Together they cover every use case — from a 120px billboard headline to a 9px meta label on a business card.

4.4

Typeface Rationale

Typography is where brand voice becomes physical. The three typefaces in this system were not chosen for fashion — they were chosen because each one does exactly one thing without compromise. All three are open-source under the SIL Open Font Licence, free for all commercial use.

Aa Bb
Cc Dd
Barlow Condensed Jeremy Tribby — Google Fonts SIL Open Font Licence — Free commercial use

The primary brand typeface. Condensed grotesque, inspired by the sign-painting tradition of the American West and the visual language of European cycling. It is disciplined, spatial, and unapologetically bold at large sizes. This is the voice of Souplesse — direct, confident, all-caps.

01 02
03 04
Space Mono Colophon Foundry — Google Fonts SIL Open Font Licence — Free commercial use

The technical voice. Monospaced, precise, machine-like. Used for all data — hex codes, measurements, race numbers, metadata labels, timestamps. Space Mono signals that something is a specification, not a headline. It brings the precision of engineering to the brand.

The ride
awaits.
Cormorant Christian Thalmann — Google Fonts SIL Open Font Licence — Free commercial use

The editorial voice. High-contrast serif with roots in classical typography. Used exclusively for long-form body text, pull quotes, and editorial moments — never for headlines or UI labels. Cormorant creates breathing room and depth, giving the brand a human dimension beyond the mechanical boldness of Barlow.

4.5

Type Hierarchy

Six levels. Each one has a defined typeface, size range, weight, and purpose. The hierarchy is rigid — mixing roles creates visual noise. A caption is never set in display weight. A headline is never set in Cormorant.

Display Barlow Condensed
Black 900
All caps
FLOW.
80–130px
Tracking −0.02em
Leading 0.88
Headline Barlow Condensed
Black 900
All caps
Every Ride Counts
40–64px
Tracking −0.02em
Leading 0.9
Subheading Barlow Condensed
Bold 700
All caps
Café Menu — Morning Rides
20–32px
Tracking +0.04em
Leading 1.1
Body Cormorant
Light 300
Sentence case
Cycling is not just sport. It is a state of mind — the place where effort and ease find the same rhythm, where the road asks a question and your legs already know the answer.
17–22px
Tracking 0
Leading 1.65
Label / UI Space Mono
Regular 400
All caps
Section 05 — Typography System
10–13px
Tracking +0.35em
Leading 2
Caption Space Mono
Regular 400
Sentence case
HEX #0052FF · RGB 0 82 255 · PANTONE 285 C
10px
Tracking +0.1em
Leading 1.9
4.6

Weight System

Barlow Condensed ships in nine weights. Souplesse uses four. Each weight has a defined role — they are not interchangeable. When in doubt, go heavier. The brand defaults to Black 900.

Black
Black 900 Primary brand weight

Wordmark, display headlines, all primary brand communication. The default weight of Souplesse — if you're not sure which to use, use this.

Extra Bold
ExtraBold 800 Secondary display

Large headlines at smaller sizes where Black can feel too heavy. Race number treatments, large-format environmental signage where legibility over distance matters.

Bold
Bold 700 Subheadings, UI

Section headers, menu category titles, UI navigation elements. The bridge between display and body — present and clear without competing with headlines.

Regular
Regular 400 Supporting text only

Used sparingly — only for longer Barlow text blocks where Black becomes too intense to read. Never for headlines. Never for the wordmark or any brand mark.

4.7

Supporting Typefaces

Barlow Condensed is the voice. Space Mono and Cormorant are its supporting cast — each one called into service for a specific context, never in competition with each other or with the primary face.

01. 02. 03.
Spec.
Space Mono Technical — Data — Precision Colophon Foundry · Google Fonts · OFL

Space Mono signals precision. Whenever a piece of information is a specification — a measurement, a code, a number, a reference — Space Mono is the right tool. Its monospaced structure means every character occupies equal space, which makes tabular data clean and scannable. It also creates a deliberate contrast against the condensed grotesque of Barlow — two completely different typographic registers in the same layout.

Use for — Eyebrows and section labels
Use for — Colour specifications and hex codes
Use for — Race numbers, distances, times
Use for — Metadata, captions, footnotes
Never for — Body text longer than two lines
Never for — Headlines or display contexts
The road asks
a question.
Cormorant Editorial — Depth — Human Christian Thalmann · Google Fonts · OFL

Cormorant is the breath between the bold. It is the typeface of the long read — menus, manifesto copy, rider stories, in-café editorial. Its high contrast and classical proportions give the brand a dimension that pure grotesque systems lack: warmth, time, and a sense that someone wrote this and meant it. Used at Light 300, always in sentence case, never forced into all-caps.

Use for — Long-form body copy, 3+ sentences
Use for — Pull quotes and editorial moments
Use for — Café menu descriptions
Use for — Brand manifesto and story text
Never for — Headlines, subheadings, UI labels
Never for — All caps or tracking above 0.05em
4.8

Typesetting Rules

How type is set matters as much as which type is used. These are the rules that govern every layout — from a social post to a printed menu.

Left alignment — preferred
Morning Ride
Espresso
A double shot. Before the legs warm up.

All brand typography defaults to left alignment. It is the most legible and the most structured — consistent with the Swiss-minimal discipline of the system.

Centre alignment — secondary
Souplesse
Cycling Café
— Est. 2026 —

Centre alignment only for lockup contexts — stacked logo variants, stamp formats, circular applications. Never for running body text.

Barlow — always all caps
Ride More.
Ride More.

Barlow Condensed is set in all caps in every brand application. The lowercase alphabet is not part of the visual system — mixed case reads as an error, not a stylistic choice.

Cormorant — never all caps
The legs remember every climb.
The legs remember every climb.

Cormorant is a classical serif designed for sentence case. All-caps strips its proportional beauty and removes the contrast that makes it useful. Never force it.

What Never To Do

SOUPLESSE
No substitute typefaces

Georgia, Helvetica, Arial, or any system font is never used in place of the defined typefaces. If the font is unavailable, fix the font — do not substitute.

SOUPLESSE
No loose tracking on display

Barlow Condensed at display sizes is always set tight at −0.02em. Wide tracking at large sizes destroys the condensed rhythm the typeface was designed around.

SOUPLESSE
No horizontal scaling

Never stretch or compress type horizontally. Barlow Condensed is already condensed — artificially widening it breaks the letterform proportions. Use the correct weight instead.

SOUPLESSE
No gradient fills in type

Type is always set in a single flat colour from the defined palette. Gradients, multi-colour fills, and photographic fills are not permitted on any brand typography.

SOUPLESSE
No drop shadows or effects

The typefaces carry their own authority. Drop shadows, glows, embossing, and bevel effects are never applied. If type needs to be legible, fix the background — not the type.

SOUPLESSE  cycling café
No mixed typefaces in one line

Barlow, Space Mono, and Cormorant occupy separate roles and separate text blocks. They never share a single line or sentence. The contrast between them works through separation, not proximity.

04 / 06 — Visual Language
Colour is a
decision.
Type is avoice.

Signal Blue and Acid Green are not decorative choices — they are commitments. Barlow Condensed is not just a font — it is how Souplesse stands in a room. Every visual rule in this section exists to make the brand unmistakable.

05 Applications

Social Media · Print · Kit · Environment

The identity in the world — across every surface it will occupy. Two parts: the social media system in full, followed by all eight physical mockups.

06 Brand Evolution
Souplesse Brand Identity 2026 · Designsbydos
Souplesse Brand Identity
Section 06

BrandEvolution

Café → Community → Team → Legacy

The identity is built to grow. What starts as a café in Johannesburg is designed to scale — across phases, across contexts, across the entire arc of what Souplesse could become.

  • 6.1Four Phases
  • 6.2Identity Scaling Rules
  • 6.3What Never Changes
6.1

Four Phases

The roadmap was set in Brand Strategy. This is the identity layer — how the visual system, the applications, and the brand presence evolve at each phase as Souplesse grows from a single café into something that changes South African cycling.

Phase 01 — Now
The
Café
Year 01 → 02
Identity at this phase

The full brand system as documented. Signal Blue and Acid Green. Barlow Condensed. The S. mark. Every touchpoint is a café touchpoint — packaging, signage, kit, social media. The identity is tight, focused, and entirely built around the physical space and the rides that leave from it.

Full colour system in use
S. mark as primary identifier
All applications café-scale
Social presence community-led
Phase 02
The
Community
Year 02 → 03
Identity at this phase

The identity expands beyond the café walls. New applications appear — event branding, youth programme materials, partnership co-marks, merchandise beyond the core kit. The wordmark begins appearing without the café descriptor as the brand becomes recognised on its own terms.

Event sub-brands under S. system
Youth programme — Carbon + Acid palette
Co-branding guidelines established
Merchandise line expands
Phase 03
The
Team
Year 03 → 05
Identity at this phase

Souplesse Racing enters the continental scene. The identity now lives on race numbers, team buses, and camera feeds. The S. mark operates at jersey scale — readable at 200km/h. Blue and Acid are the team colours. The café is the origin story, not the main character anymore.

Race kit system — strict Blue + Acid
Team bus, vehicle, equipment branding
Sponsor co-mark placement rules
S. mark — monochrome white only on kit
Phase 04
The
Legacy
Year 05 +
Identity at this phase

World Tour ambition. The identity is now a global sports brand with roots in a single café in Johannesburg. The wordmark stands alone — no descriptor needed. The origin story is the point of difference. No other World Tour team started as a cycling café. That is the brand.

Wordmark without descriptor — earned
Multi-location café sub-brand system
Global race presence — same S. mark
The door is still open at 05:30
6.2

Identity Scaling Rules

As the brand grows, so does the pressure on the identity system. These rules govern how Souplesse scales without losing what makes it Souplesse.

Rule 01
The Mark
Never Changes

The S. mark is fixed. No matter how large the organisation grows — one café or a World Tour team — the mark stays exactly as specified. Brands that redesign their mark when they grow up are announcing that they were unsure of themselves when they started. Souplesse is not unsure.

Rule 02
Colour Before
Everything

Signal Blue and Acid Green are the most recognisable assets the brand owns. At Phase 3 and beyond, when the team appears on broadcast television and international race coverage, those two colours are what people see first — before they can read the name. Protect them absolutely. Never dilute with additional brand colours.

Rule 03
Sub-brands
Sit Below

Any sub-brand — the youth programme, a race event, a second location — sits visually subordinate to the Souplesse master brand. It borrows from the system: the typeface, the colour palette, the grid logic. It does not introduce new colours, new marks, or new typographic voices. The family is coherent or it is nothing.

Rule 04
The Descriptor
Is Earned Away

Right now the wordmark carries "Cycling Café" beneath it. That descriptor is dropped when the brand no longer needs it — when Souplesse is recognised without explanation. This is not a decision made by the design team. It is a decision made by the audience. When they stop needing the descriptor, remove it.

Rule 05
Acid Is Always
the Exception

At every phase, Acid Green remains the accent — never the ground, except for race day and event-specific materials. The moment Acid becomes the default, it stops being a signal. Its power is its rarity. A World Tour team that overuses its accent colour looks like a brand that forgot what it was.

Rule 06
The Origin
Is the Asset

At Phase 4, every competitor is a professional sports team. None of them started as a cycling café in Johannesburg that opened its doors at 05:30 so riders had somewhere to go. That story is irreplaceable. It belongs in every press release, every sponsorship deck, every piece of brand communication. It is the thing no budget can buy.

6.3

What Never Changes

Phases change. Scale changes. Applications change. These do not.

Fixed — In perpetuity
Signal Blue
#0052FF
The primary colour of the brand at every scale. Never adjusted for trend, never swapped for a different blue. This exact hex. Always.
Acid Green
#CCFF00
The accent that makes the brand visible from a peloton at 50km/h. Never replaced, never softened. The terminal E is always this colour.
The S.
Mark
Proportions, spacing, the Acid dot — all fixed. The mark scales but never reshapes. It is the same mark on a sugar stick as on a team bus.
Barlow
Condensed 900
The wordmark weight is fixed. Every headline uses this cut. No other weight replaces it in display contexts, regardless of layout or platform.
The Terminal
E in Acid
The one consistent brand signal across every single application — from Phase 1 to Phase 4. The E is always Acid. This never changes.
Flexible — Adapts with context
Descriptor
Cycling Café is used until the brand is recognised without it. Removed at Phase 3 or 4, by audience consensus — not internal decision.
Applications
New touchpoints at each phase — race vehicles, event collateral, second-location signage. Each uses the system, adds nothing outside of it.
Sub-brands
Youth programme, events, and location sub-brands are permitted within the system. Each one borrows; none compete.
Carbon vs
Off-White
Background colour shifts with context. Race applications lean Carbon and Blue. Print and editorial applications may use Off-White. Both are correct.
Voice
Register
Tone shifts from intimate café voice to authoritative sports brand voice as scale increases. The principles remain — the register adapts.
06 / 06 — Brand Evolution
Start here.
Go everywhere.

Every great sports brand has an origin that is more interesting than its current scale. For Souplesse, that origin is a café in Johannesburg, a door open at 05:30, and the belief that cycling is for everyone. That is not a phase. That is the whole story — told at whatever size the world is ready for.
